narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, looking at factors like grades, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 37 (slightly above the 36.70 average), Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at the bottom 10th percentile (far below typical mid-range positions around the 5th or 6th decile), middle family income bracket, and plan to attend full-time—and predicted a 69.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school decile ranking. Most critically, your low Year 3 decile—placing you in the bottom 10% of your school—strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure, signaling weaker late-stage academic performance compared to peers who typically rank in the middle of their classes. Your LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since it's a bit stronger than average and shows solid testing ability. Meanwhile, your equally low Year 1 decile reinforced the failure risk, highlighting early struggles that deviate from the stronger starts most successful students have. Overall, these early and late undergraduate performance indicators outweighed your decent LSAT, especially since full-time status is common and didn't sway things much. While your middle-income background aligns with many applicants, it didn't offset the academic red flags that set you apart from typical admitted students who pass the bar. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it further or addressing those low deciles by pursuing postgraduate courses or targeted academic improvement to rebuild your record. Consider community college or targeted prep programs that have helped similar students demonstrate growth. Reapplying after showing upward trends could flip the model's view of your bar prospects. With persistence, you can reshape this story for future applications.
